data.title: I Squared confirms deal to buy stake in Matterhorn gas pipeline with Enbridge, MPLX
data.description:
I Squared Capital, together with Enbridge (NYSE:ENB) and MPLX (NYSE:MPLX), confirmed Thursday an agreement to buy a significant equity interest in the Matterhorn Express natural gas pipeline; the purchase price was not disclosed but speculation had said a deal could value Matterhorn at more than $5B including debt.

Under the deal terms, I Squared will acquire ~37% of Matterhorn through its ISQ Global Infrastructure Fund III, alongside infrastructure company WhiteWater Midstream and infrastructure investment firm FIC; following the close of the transaction, the pipeline will be owned 65% by MXP HoldCo, 15% by ONEOK (OKE), 10% by Enbridge (NYSE:ENB) and 10% by MPLX (NYSE:MPLX); WhiteWater Midstream, through MXP HoldCo, will continue to operate Matterhorn.

The 510-mile Matterhorn pipeline was placed into service in November 2024 and has ~2.5B cf/day of fully contracted capacity; it transports natural gas from the Waha area in the Permian Basin to Wharton, Texas, with additional delivery capabilities into the Katy, Texas area.

data.title: Wall Street rally loses some steam; Dow seesaws as Cisco surge offsets Walmart weakness
data.description:
Wall Street was muted on Thursday, as a combination of buyer exhaustion and some lackluster economic data weighed on sentiment. U.S. Treasury yields fell as traders snapped up bonds.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last -0.05% at 5,889.59 points in morning trade, while the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -0.39% at 19,071.53 points. The blue-chip Dow (DJI) was +0.04% at 42,068.44 points.

A three-day rally sparked by a surprise trade deal between the U.S. and China and a benign consumer inflation report lost some steam. 

"The strong recent equity rally showed some signs of exhaustion over the past 24 hours, even though gains for the tech mega caps helped the S&P 500 (+0.10%) extend its gains since the closing low on April 7 to +18.26%," Deutsche Bank's Peter Sidorov said. 

Earlier, before the opening bell on Thursday, investors received a slew of economic indicators. First up was the April producer price index (PPI) report, with both headline and core producer prices unexpectedly declining on a M/M basis. 

At the same time, U.S. Census Bureau said retail sales in April edged up +0.1% M/M, just above the consensus for a flat reading. Core retail sales also increased by a similar amount, but came in below the expected rise.   

"Because it largely represents goods spending, which comprises less than a third of overall personal consumption in the United States, the retail sales report is not typically an A-lister among economic indicators…until now," Wells Fargo's Tim Quinlan said. 

"For the next few months at least, the retail sales report is set to be center stage as we seek to measure the impact of tariffs. While import duties can introduce pass-through effect to the service sector, it is goods that are apt to be the most impacted. We may eventually see these impacts manifested through product scarcity or higher prices, but the first action will simply be evident in the sales activity," Quinlan added. 

Additionally, two measures of manufacturing activity continued to show weakness in the sector. 

Also weighing on sentiment on Thursday was Walmart (WMT), -1.6%. The U.S. retailing giant's management on an earnings conference call warned that tariffs could lead to higher prices for consumers. 

Offsetting some of Walmart's (WMT) weakness was Cisco (CSCO). Shares of the networking giant were +6.7% after its solid quarterly results and guidance.  

Turning to the fixed-income markets, U.S. Treasury yields were lower. The benchmark 10-year yield (US10Y) was down 7 basis points to 4.47%, while the shorter-end more rate-sensitive 2-year yield (US2Y) was down 6 basis points to 3.99%. 

For more, see how Treasury yields have done across the curve on the Seeking Alpha bond page.

data.title: Caesars added as a new long idea at Hedgeye
data.description:
Caesars Entertainment (NASDAQ:CZR) was added as a new long idea at Hedgeye. 

"We think CZR bears are overstaying their welcome," Hedgeye analyst Sean Jenkins wrote in a note on Thursday. "Our work suggest consensus is: 1) too bearish on near-term GGR growth around the US; 2) much too bearish on CZR’s position within the regional & online gaming ecosystems; 3) ascribing little to no value to their omni-channel growth business."

Shares of Caesars (NASDAQ:CZR) fell 2% on Thursday despite the bullish note. Caesar's short interest is 13%.

"CZR appears cheap on numbers that could (finally) be moving higher," Jenkins added. "Cheap is not catalyst, but the rate-of-change in top line trends, EBITDA revisions, leverage, and shareholder returns certainly are (especially against a backdrop of multi-year highs in short interest)."

data.title: IS&S outlines over 30% revenue and EBITDA growth target for 2025 while expanding military and commercial operations
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Innovative Solutions and Support, Inc. (ISSC) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Shahram Askarpour reported "growth in revenue of just over 100%, driven by momentum from our new military programs, including significant growth from our F-16 program and contributions from our legacy platform." He highlighted improved trends in the commercial business, especially in the air transport segment, with EBITDA up over 200% and profit up more than 300% year-over-year.
  • Askarpour unveiled ongoing investments in infrastructure and systems capabilities to support defense customers and noted the completed ERP system integration, expanded IT infrastructure, and strengthened security and accounting services for DFARS compliance. "During the second quarter, we completed the integration of our ERP system, we further expanded our more robust IT infrastructure and strengthen our security and accounting services to make us compliant with Defense Federal Acquisition Regulation Supplement, or DFARS, requirements."
  • Askarpour updated that the Exton, Pennsylvania facility expansion is on track, aiming to triple production capabilities by mid-2025, and emphasized the integration progress of the Honeywell acquisition, which is contributing duplicative costs during the transition.
  • The company continues to target acquisitions: "Deploying capital for strategic acquisitions remain a key priority. Although our most recent acquisitions have been focused on complementary product lines from larger avionics suppliers, we continue to evaluate opportunities to acquire small avionics manufacturers where we anticipate synergies will be realized by incorporating their outsourced production in our facility."
  • CFO Jeff DiGiovanni stated: "We generated net revenues of $21.9 million in the second quarter, more than double our revenues during the second quarter last year. The increase was driven primarily by contribution from the recently acquired Honeywell military product line, which contributed $10.8 million and growth in our air transport market."

Outlook

  • Askarpour reiterated, "We continue to expect at least 40% of our revenue to come from military customers during fiscal 2025 and we are excited by our progress and the opportunities that lie ahead of our military business."
  • Management affirmed the fiscal year goal: "We remain on track to deliver on our goal to generate both revenue and EBITDA growth of greater than 30% when compared to fiscal year 2024."
  • No explicit change in guidance from last quarter; the over 30% growth target remains.

Financial Results

  • Net revenues for Q2 totaled $21.9 million. Product sales were $13.2 million and service revenue was $8.8 million, with $3 million of the latter from the F-16 program.
  • Gross profit was $11.3 million, with a gross margin of 51.4%, up sequentially from 41.4% in Q1 but slightly down from 52% a year ago. DiGiovanni explained, "We generated more normalized gross margins under our Honeywell contracts, which was the main driver of improved gross margin relative to the prior quarter."
  • Operating expense was $4.3 million, representing 19.6% of revenue, down from 36.7% a year ago.
  • Net income for the quarter reached $5.3 million, with GAAP earnings per share of $0.30. EBITDA was $7.6 million.
  • Backlog as of March 31 was $80 million. Cash flow from operations was $1.3 million; free cash flow was negative $300,000 due to facility expansion. Net debt at quarter-end stood at $26.2 million.

Q&A

  • Gowshi Sri, Singular Research: Asked about the magnitude of Honeywell product line pull-forward and guidance stability. Askarpour responded, "We don't anticipate further delays post transition... In terms of our guidance that we gave for this year, I think, we're well into over 30% growth as we see it."
  • Sri followed up on air transport revenue and commercial retrofit pipeline. Askarpour said demand is high due to new airplane production delays: "We're seeing benefits of that. And in the foreseeable future, we'll see -- we hope that that's going to -- that trend is going to continue."
  • Sri inquired about gross margin stability. Askarpour stated, "Gross margins are very volatile and kind of lumpy... we've been trying to steer everybody away from gross margins and put a focus on EBITDA margins and profit margins."
  • Doug Ruth, Lenox Financial Services: Asked for DoD sales percentage. DiGiovanni provided, "$21.9 million in sales, $10.3 million was associated with the F-16 piece... at least 40% is military, as we're experiencing."
  • Ruth asked about hiring. DiGiovanni replied, "Very, very little," with Askarpour noting ongoing search for talent but F-16 platform hiring is complete.
  • Ruth requested update on clean room expansion. Askarpour explained the process is routine and "under control."
  • Ruth asked about the next-generation utility management system. Askarpour responded, "They're still planning on doing some flight testing on it over the next month."
  • Andrew Rem, Odinson Partners: Clarified revenue breakdown from Honeywell acquisition. DiGiovanni confirmed, "$10.8 million in total, of which $3 million was customer service, so the delta is $7.8 million is product."
  • Rem inquired about CapEx, D&A normalization, and Q3 pull-forward effects. DiGiovanni confirmed $6 million CapEx for the year and stated D&A should normalize after valuation period closes next quarter; pull-forward magnitude for Q3 is hard to predict.
  • Rem asked about Q4 revenue outlook. Askarpour replied, "I don't expect a meaningful decline... we have $80 million in backlog as of end of March."
  • Sri asked about ERP system productivity and SG&A trends. DiGiovanni said improvements are expected but it's too soon to quantify P&L impact; SG&A expected in the $3-4 million range quarterly.
  • Sri queried about Exton facility utilization. DiGiovanni stated, "the 30% growth is based on everything that we have here today and it really excludes a lot that the new building could produce."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts displayed a positive to slightly positive tone, congratulating management for strong results and seeking details on growth sustainability, margin volatility, and integration progress.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic outlook, using phrases such as "we are confident we are strategically positioned to continue generating profitable growth" and "we remain on track to deliver on our goal to generate both revenue and EBITDA growth of greater than 30%." During Q&A, management was transparent about volatility and transition risks but reiterated confidence in execution.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone shifted from cautious optimism to stronger confidence, while analysts remained supportive but probed for clarity around operational risks and margins.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for over 30% revenue and EBITDA growth was reiterated; no explicit change from Q1.
  • Strategic focus remains on military expansion, commercial growth, and integration of acquisitions, with progress on ERP and facility expansion highlighted this quarter.
  • Key metrics improved: revenue, gross profit, and net income all rose sharply compared to Q1, with gross margin recovering sequentially.
  • Analysts continued to focus on military/commercial mix, margin sustainability, and acquisition integration, but with increased attention on operational leverage and facility expansion.
  • Management’s confidence was more pronounced this quarter, emphasizing scalability and competitive positioning.

Risks and Concerns

  • Integration of Honeywell product lines continues to create duplicative costs and margin variability.
  • Ongoing facility expansion and clean room construction present execution risks, though management described these as routine.
  • Military program revenue concentration could impact margin stability due to lower gross margins, as acknowledged by management.
  • Supply chain uncertainties during the Honeywell transition could affect short-term revenue recognition and backlog execution.
  • Management highlighted that gross margin volatility should be expected, urging focus on EBITDA and profit margins.

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized that IS&S delivered substantial year-over-year growth in the second quarter, supported by military and commercial momentum, successful execution on strategic acquisitions, and ongoing investments in operational infrastructure. The company remains committed to a disciplined capital allocation framework and expects to achieve over 30% growth in both revenue and EBITDA for the full fiscal year, backed by a robust backlog and expanding production capacity. The integration of recent acquisitions and facility expansion positions IS&S for continued profitable growth while management maintains a confident outlook for the remainder of fiscal 2025.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
